summary:
The Philistines were one of the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]] who settled along the southern coastal plain of Canaan following the disruptions of the Bronze Age Collapse around 1175 BC. Egyptian records from the reign of Ramesses III describe their defeat at the Nile Delta, after which they appear to have been permitted or forced to settle in the region of Canaan under Egyptian oversight. The five principal Philistine cities — Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, and Gath — formed a loose political confederation known in the Bible as the Pentapolis.

Archaeological excavations at Philistine sites have revealed a distinctive material culture that blends Aegean traditions with local Canaanite practices, supporting the view that the Philistines originated in the Aegean world, possibly Cyprus or the Greek mainland. Their early pottery styles closely resemble Mycenaean and Cypriot forms, and their initial settlements show architectural features consistent with Aegean building traditions. Over subsequent centuries, however, Philistine culture became increasingly Semitized through interaction with surrounding Canaanite and Israelite populations.

The Philistines feature prominently in the early books of the Hebrew Bible as recurring adversaries of the [[event:62|Kingdom of Israel]], with conflicts including the capture of the Ark of the Covenant, the career of Samson, and the famous duel between David and Goliath. Iron-working technology, which the Philistines may have possessed at an advantage over neighboring peoples in the early Iron Age, contributed to their military effectiveness. Philistine political independence ended with Assyrian conquest in the late eighth and early seventh centuries BC, and as a distinct ethnic identity they gradually disappeared from the historical record.
